items.title: Always pair hidden Linear layers with nonlinearity
items.avoid: Chaining `Linear → Linear → Linear` expecting depth alone to add expressivity.
items.prefer: Pattern `Linear → ReLU → Linear → …` for hidden tiers; linear output for regression or raw logits for classification.
items.why: Nonlinearity between hidden tiers is what enables non-linear decision boundaries — the core reason MLPs beat single-layer models.
items.example:
```python
layers = [nn.Linear(d_in, 128), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(128, 64), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(64, n_classes)]
```

items.title: Start with one hidden layer before going deep
items.avoid: Defaulting to four hidden layers on a small tabular dataset without ablation.
items.prefer: Try one hidden layer with moderate width (32–256), validate, then add depth only if underfitting persists.
items.why: Each hidden layer adds parameters and optimization complexity; shallow MLPs often match performance with easier training.
items.example: Churn model: compare `40→32→1` vs `40→32→16→8→1` on the same validation split; keep the simpler model if AUC is within noise.

items.title: Match output head to supervised task
items.avoid: Using ten sigmoid outputs for mutually exclusive 10-class MNIST labels.
items.prefer: Multi-class: K linear logits + `CrossEntropyLoss`; binary: one logit + BCE or two logits; regression: one linear output + MSE.
items.why: The MLP hidden stack builds features; the output head must align with the loss so gradients teach the right boundary shape.
items.example: MNIST: `nn.Linear(64, 10)` logits with `nn.CrossEntropyLoss()` — no softmax module required in the forward pass.
